text:
RIVERSIDE NEUROLOGY CLINIC

Clinic Date: 12 September 2025

Dr Alex Turner
Maple Grove Medical Practice

12 Maple Grove, Brixton, London SW2 3AB

Dear Dr Alex Turner,

Jordan Ellis, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: H937421, NHS No. 7439205618
Flat 3, 28 Riverside Walk, London SE15 4LP

Diagnoses/summary: Drug-resistant focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset. Prior EEGs with intermittent left temporal sharp activity; prior telemetry inconclusive. No history of generalised tonic–clonic seizures in the last year.

Imaging: MRI brain (2018) reported as normal; no definite epileptogenic lesion identified.

Previous antiseizure medications: levetiracetam (mood irritability, persistent events), carbamazepine (dizziness, hyponatraemia), lamotrigine (partial response only), valproate (weight gain, discontinued), lacosamide (ataxia), clobazam PRN (sedation), vigabatrin (visual symptoms, stopped). Not tried to date: topiramate, zonisamide, perampanel, pregabalin, tiagabine, rufinamide.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

I reviewed Jordan Ellis in clinic today, who attended alone. They report focal aware seizures occurring every day, typically brief rising epigastric sensations followed by déjà vu and word-finding difficulty lasting 30–60 seconds, with occasional progression to focal impaired awareness episodes approximately once weekly. Triggers noted include sleep deprivation and missed meals. There are no recent injuries or hospitalisations. They continue to work part-time and keep a seizure diary via a smartphone app.

Current medications: lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily; sertraline 50 mg once daily; folic acid 5 mg once daily; omeprazole 20 mg once daily.

Examination today was unremarkable. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were normal. Neurological examination showed normal cranial nerves, power, coordination, and gait. Blood pressure 118/74, weight stable. Routine safety bloods in July were within reference ranges (including sodium and liver profile).

Discussion: We discussed management options for persistent focal epilepsy, including trial of an additional antiseizure medication (topiramate or zonisamide), non-pharmacological measures (sleep regularity, structured meals, stress management), and re-evaluation for device therapy if inadequate response. Risks and benefits of add-on therapy were explained, including cognitive effects, mood change, nephrolithiasis risk (for zonisamide), and weight change.

Plan:
- Initiate topiramate 25 mg nightly for 2 weeks, then 25 mg twice daily if tolerated; further slow titration planned based on response and side effects. Written information provided. Advise good hydration and to report visual symptoms promptly.
- Continue lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily for now; consider gradual reduction if topiramate proves effective.
- Arrange repeat EEG with sleep deprivation to better characterise interictal activity.
- Seizure safety reinforced; continue seizure diary documenting events occurring every day, specifying time, trigger, and impact on function.
- Telephone follow-up with epilepsy nurse in 4–6 weeks; clinic review in 3 months or earlier if concerns.

Thank you for your ongoing care of Jordan Ellis. Please do not hesitate to contact me if there are any queries or if medication adjustments are needed between appointments.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: focal aware seizures occurring every day

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states: 'They report focal aware seizures occurring every day... with occasional progression to focal impaired awareness episodes approximately once weekly.' Per the rule of selecting the highest frequency, daily focal aware seizures represent the highest frequency. Convert 'every day' to '1 per day'. No need to sum because the weekly events are less frequent and represent progression of the same condition.
seizure_frequency_number: focal aware seizures occurring every day
